title: Allergic Rhinitis
oneLiner: IgE-mediated nasal inflammation to inhaled allergens; affects ~20% of people, linked to asthma, sinusitis, and impaired quality of life.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:**
A 24-year-old medical student presents in late April with two weeks of sneezing, clear rhinorrhea, bilateral nasal itching, and watery eyes. Symptoms are worst in the morning and when outdoors. They have no fever, no purulent discharge, and a history of eczema as a child. On exam, you see “allergic shiners” (dark circles under the eyes) and a transverse nasal crease from rubbing their nose upward — the “allergic salute.”

**Dissecting the vignette:**

- **Seasonal timing (late April)** → tree pollen (birch, oak) trigger; points to seasonal allergic rhinitis (SAR)
- **Bilateral symptoms** → indicates systemic hypersensitivity, not local structural problem
- **Clear rhinorrhea + sneezing** → histamine-mediated hypersecretion and nerve irritation
- **Itchy eyes** → ocular allergy often co-occurs (allergic conjunctivitis)
- **Morning predominance** → overnight allergen exposure (pillow dust mites) + diurnal cortisol dip enhances inflammation
- **Eczema history** → atopic march: eczema → food allergy → asthma → allergic rhinitis
- **Allergic shiners & nasal crease** → chronic nasal congestion → venous stasis (shiners) + repeated upward rubbing (crease)

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

1. **Isolated cough** — without sneezing or rhinorrhea; often misdiagnosed as post-nasal drip or asthma; clue: cough worse with allergen exposure, bilateral wheeze may be absent.
2. **Perennial symptoms with no seasonal pattern** — dust mite or mold allergy; patient thinks they have “chronic colds”; clue: symptoms persist year-round, worse in bedrooms, improve on holiday away from home.
3. **Nasal congestion only** — no itching, no sneezing; mimics vasomotor rhinitis or structural obstruction; clue: response to nasal steroids but not antihistamines.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:**
Allergic rhinitis is an inflammation of the inside of the nose triggered by an overreaction of the immune system to normally harmless particles in the air — like pollen, dust mites, or pet dander. This causes sneezing, a runny or blocked nose, and itching.

**Why it matters clinically:**

- **Frequency:** Affects up to 20-30% of adults and 40% of children in Western countries — commonest chronic disease in paediatrics.
- **Stakes:** Not “just hay fever.” It associates with asthma (two-thirds of asthmatics have rhinitis), sleep disturbance, impaired concentration, school/work absenteeism, and can worsen sinusitis, otitis media, and nasal polyps.
- **Common pitfalls:** Mistaking for recurrent viral infections leading to unnecessary antibiotics; over-prescribing oral decongestants that cause rebound congestion; failing to escalate to immunotherapy in patients with asthma or poor QoL.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**Story of disease development:**

1. **Sensitization phase (asymptomatic):** Inhalant allergen (e.g., birch pollen protein) is processed by dendritic cells in nasal mucosa → presented to naïve T-cells → Th2 differentiation → IL-4, IL-13 production → B-cell class-switching to IgE → IgE binds to mast cells and basophils.

2. **Early-phase response (minutes):** Re-exposure to same allergen cross-links surface IgE on mast cells → degranulation → release of histamine, tryptase, prostaglandins, leukotrienes.
   - **Mechanism → Sign:**
     - Histamine on H1 receptors in sensory nerves → *sneezing, itching*
     - Histamine on nasal glands + vasodilation → *profuse clear rhinorrhea*
     - Vasodilation + increased permeability → *congestion, edema*

3. **Late-phase response (4-6 hours):** Chemotaxis of eosinophils, neutrophils, T-cells → ongoing inflammation → epithelial damage, eosinophil activation → *persistent congestion, hyperresponsiveness* (symptoms triggered by non-allergic stimuli like cold air or perfume).

**Connecting to pattern recognition cues:**

- **Bilateral symptoms** → because allergen contacting both nasal cavities simultaneously; systemic IgE sensitization not unilateral.
- **Seasonal onset** → reflects short-lived pollen seasons; perennial allergens (dust mites) present year-round but worsen with humidity.
- **Morning worsening** → dust mites from bedding; also circadian variation in cortisol (low early morning) amplifies mast cell responsiveness.
- **Allergic shiners** → chronic venous congestion from nasal edema impairing periorbital venous drainage.
- **Nasal crease** → repeated “allergic salute” — mechanical trauma from rubbing upward.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Common Triggers | Stem Clue to Identify |
|----------|----------------|-----------------------|
| **Seasonal** | Tree pollen (spring), grass pollen (summer), weed pollen (autumn) | Symptoms appear only in specific months; travel to different region changes symptoms. |
| **Perennial** | House dust mites (Dermatophagoides pteronyssinus), moulds (Aspergillus, Alternaria), cockroach droppings, pet dander (cat, dog) | Year-round symptoms, worse in bedroom/bathroom (mould) or after cleaning; improve on holiday away from home. |
| **Occupational** | Flour (bakers), wood dust (carpenters), latex, isocyanates (painters), lab animals | Symptoms at work, resolve on weekends/holidays. |
| **Co-factor triggers** | Strong odours (perfume, cigarette smoke), cold air, exercise — these provoke symptoms in already inflamed mucosa (non-allergic hyperreactivity) | Patient says “I react to everything” — suggests late-phase inflammation is established. |

**Most common cause overall:** House dust mites (especially in humid climates). For seasonal, grass pollen is the top trigger in many regions.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
**ARIA (Allergic Rhinitis and its Impact on Asthma) classification** — split by duration and severity:
- **Intermittent** (<4 days/week or <4 weeks) vs **Persistent** (≥4 days/week and ≥4 weeks)
- **Mild** (normal sleep, no impairment of daily activities) vs **Moderate-Severe** (abnormal sleep, impairment of work/school/leisure)

No formal scoring threshold; diagnosis is clinical + confirmatory testing.

**Key tests:**

| Test | What It Shows | Why Order It | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|--------------|----------------|
| **Skin Prick Test (SPT)** | Immediate wheal-and-flare to allergen extract applied to forearm | Confirms specific IgE sensitisation; cheap, quick, highly sensitive | Wheal ≥3 mm than negative control = positive. Must use positive (histamine) and negative (saline) controls. Interpret with clinical history — sensitisation may not equal disease. |
| **Serum specific IgE (RAST/ImmunoCAP)** | Quantity of IgE to a specific allergen | When skin testing unavailable, or if severe eczema/dermographism prevents SPT | >0.35 kU/L positive; higher levels correlate with stronger clinical reactivity but not linear. |
| **Nasal smear for eosinophils** | Eosinophils >10% of total cells on stained smear | Points to allergic inflammation rather than infective or vasomotor rhinitis | Positive strongly supports allergic rhinitis; negative does not rule out. |
| **CT sinuses** | Mucosal thickening, blockage of osteomeatal complex | Only if suspicion of chronic rhinosinusitis or complications (polyps, sinusitis) | Not first-line for uncomplicated rhinitis. |

**Pattern-recognition single finding that confirms:**
A positive SPT to a relevant allergen *combined with* symptom diary showing temporal correlation (e.g., positive grass pollen SPT + symptoms in June) is diagnostic. **Single finding** that strongly supports: nasal smear with eosinophils — but not 100% sensitive.

**What rules out:** Absence of any IgE sensitisation (negative SPT + negative specific IgE to all common allergens) makes allergic rhinitis very unlikely → consider non-allergic rhinitis, structural causes.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour — acute exacerbation or first presentation):**
- **Oral antihistamine:** Cetirizine 10 mg PO once daily (or loratadine 10 mg PO, less sedating). *Why:* H1-blockade rapidly reduces sneezing, itching, rhinorrhea within 1 hour. Use for 7-14 days as needed; start with second-generation to avoid drowsiness.
- **Intranasal corticosteroid (INCS):** Fluticasone furoate 55 mcg (2 sprays each nostril) once daily. *Why:* Most effective for congestion and late-phase inflammation. Takes days to work, but start immediately for EARLY control. If symptoms are mild, may start antihistamine first.

**What NOT to do in the first hour:** Do not give a topical decongestant (e.g., oxymetazoline) for >3 days — causes rebound rhinitis medicamentosa. Do not start antibiotics.

**Ward (24-72h — if hospitalized for exacerbation, e.g., with asthma flare):**
- **Intranasal corticosteroids** (continue above)
- **Saline nasal irrigation** (e.g., NeilMed sinus rinse) 2-4 times daily — mechanically clears allergens, reduces mucus viscosity, improves INCS penetration.
- **Add leukotriene receptor antagonist** (montelukast 10 mg PO nocte) if patient has concomitant asthma or inadequate symptom control after 2 weeks of INCS alone.
- **Consider oral corticosteroid** (prednisolone 30-40 mg PO daily for 5 days) only in severe cases with massive polyps or acute sinusitis. *Why:* reduces severe inflammation, but risks systemic side effects.

**Long-term (outpatient, chronic management):**
- **Stepwise escalation ladder:**  
  1. Allergen avoidance (mite-proof pillow/mattress covers, dehumidifier <50%, remove carpet, keep pets out of bedroom).  
  2. First-line prophylactic therapy: intranasal corticosteroid (fluticasone furoate 55 mcg – 2 sprays per nostril daily, or mometasone furoate 100 mcg – 2 sprays each nostril daily).  
  3. If inadequate response after 2-4 weeks: add oral antihistamine (e.g., cetirizine 10 mg daily) or intranasal antihistamine (azelastine 1 spray each nostril twice daily — works faster than INCS for itch/sneeze).  
  4. If still uncontrolled: consider immunotherapy (sublingual tablets or subcutaneous injections) for specific allergens (grass, dust mite, ragweed). *Why:* modifies immune response, induces tolerance, can cure disease. Duration 3-5 years.  
- **Ophthalmic allergy:** Olopatadine 0.1% eye drops (1 drop in each eye BD) for itch, redness.

**Common pitfalls to avoid:**
- Using oral antihistamines alone for congestion — they do little for obstruction. INCS is best.
- Prescribing decongestant sprays for >7 days | leads to drug-induced rhinitis.
- Forgetting to assess for asthma in every patient with allergic rhinitis — recommend peak flow diary and spirometry.
- Not escalating to immunotherapy in patients with poor QoL or new-onset asthma despite pharmacotherapy.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Allergic Rhinitis | Non-Allergic Rhinitis (Vasomotor) | Chronic Rhinosinusitis (CRS) | Nasal Polyps |
|---------|-------------------|-----------------------------------|------------------------------|--------------|
| **Itching** | Prominent (nose, eyes, palate) | Minimal or absent | Usually absent | Absent |
| **Sneezing** | Frequent, paroxysmal | Occasional | Uncommon | Rare |
| **Rhinorrhea** | Clear, watery | Clear or serous | Thick, purulent possible | Watery or thick |
| **Nasal obstruction** | Mild-moderate, fluctuates | Often prominent (trigger-dependent) | Moderate-severe, fixed | Severe, bilateral, progressive |
| **Seasonal pattern** | Yes (allergen-specific) | No (triggered by irritants: smoke, cold air, heat) | No | No |
| **Facial pain/pressure** | Rare | Rare | Common (over maxillary sinuses) | May occur with sinusitis |
| **Olfactory loss** | Mild, reversible | Mild | Moderate | Prominent, often early |
| **Response to antihistamines** | Good (early-phase sx) | Poor | Poor | Poor |
| **Response to nasal steroids** | Good | Variable | Good (as adjunct) | Good (reduces size) |
| **Key diagnostic test** | Skin prick test + specific IgE positive | Negative allergy tests; challenge with external triggers | CT sinuses (mucosal thickening, air-fluid levels) | CT sinuses (soft-tissue density filling nasal cavity); endoscopy shows grape-like masses |
| **Key differentiator** | Itching + sneezing + allergen-specific IgE | Absence of itching, no eosinophilia on smear | Purulent drainage + facial pain + CT findings | Anosmia + visible polyps on exam |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**1. Acute Sinusitis**
- **Presentation:** Persistent purulent nasal discharge, unilateral facial pain over maxillary/ frontal sinuses, fever, headache worse on bending.
- **Key signs:** Tenderness on percussion over sinuses, nasal endoscopy showing pus from middle meatus.
- **What to do:** Treat with amoxicillin 500 mg TDS PO for 7-10 days (or co-amoxiclav if previous antibiotics). Add saline irrigation, mucolytics. If recurrent, refer ENT.

**2. Asthma Exacerbation**
- **Presentation:** Deteriorating peak flow, nocturnal cough, wheeze, chest tightness during pollen season.
- **Key signs:** Bilateral expiratory wheeze, reduced FEV1/FVC ratio <0.7.
- **What to do:** Optimize rhinitis treatment (INCS + antihistamine) → the nose controls the lower airway. In acute: β2-agonist (salbutamol 2 puffs PRN every 20 minutes for 2 hours, then as needed) + oral prednisolone 40 mg daily for 5 days. If severe, escalate to senior.

**3. Otitis Media with Effusion (Glue Ear)**
- **Presentation:** Hearing loss, ear fullness, delayed speech in children; not painful.
- **Key signs:** Dull tympanic membrane, reduced mobility on pneumatic otoscopy, B-type tympanogram.
- **What to do:** Treat rhinitis aggressively (INCS, antihistamine). If persists >3 months, refer ENT for grommets.

**4. Nasal Polyps (late-presentation pattern)**
- **Pattern:** Patient with long-standing untreated allergic rhinitis (or non-allergic eosinophilic disease) develops progressive anosmia, constant nasal obstruction not responding to decongestants; exam shows pale greyish, grape-like masses.
- **What to do:** Start INCS high dose (e.g., betamethasone nose drops or fluticasone PPP). If no improvement, refer ENT for polypectomy and consider aspirin desensitization if associated with aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease.

**5. Sleep Apnoea / Impaired QoL**
- Chronic congestion → mouth breathing → obstructive sleep apnoea (snoring, daytime somnolence). Treat rhinitis → improve CPAP compliance.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

1. **"ARIA" classification** — *A*llergic *R*hinitis *I*mpact on *A*sthma → severity: Mild, Moderate-Severe; duration: Intermittent, Persistent.
2. **"SPIKES"** for symptoms: *S*neezing, *P*runny nose (rhinorrhea), *I*tching (nose, eyes, palate), *K*ongestion (nasal obstruction), *E*ye watering, *S*mell loss (partial).
3. **Common seasonal allergens by month** (Northern temperate): "Tree in Spring, Grass in Summer, Weed in Fall" — mnemonic: **T**ree, **G**rass, **W**eed → **T**he **G**arden **W**ilts.

**High-yield clinical pearls:**

- **The “allergic salute”** — upward rubbing of the nose with palm, causing a transverse nasal crease (Dennie-Morgan line variant). Seen in children and adults with chronic rhinitis.
- **Allergic shiners** — dark periorbital circles due to venous stasis from nasal congestion; not pathognomonic but strongly supportive of allergic rhinitis.
- **The “hourglass” pattern of congestion** — alternating nasal obstruction between nostrils (nasal cycle) is exaggerated in allergic rhinitis; if obstruction is fixed, suspect structural cause (deviated septum, polyps).
- **Ocular involvement** — if a patient denies eye symptoms but sneezes profusely, ask “do your eyes water or itch?” often they didn't think to mention it because it’s mild.
- **“Allergy skin test” is diagnostic, but interpret cautiously** — + test + history = disease; + test without symptoms = sensitization without clinical allergy (common in atopic individuals).
- **The key message for patients:** “Treat the nose early, and you can improve your asthma and prevent sinus infections.”
- **When to suspect non-allergic rhinitis:** no itching, no sneezing, no eye symptoms, negative allergy tests, trigger is irritant (e.g., perfume, cold air, spicy food). Treatment: intranasal ipratropium for rhinorrhea or intranasal azelastine/fluticasone combination.

**Common exam / ward mistakes to avoid:**

- **Missing asthma** in a rhinitis patient — do not assume “just hay fever”; ask about nocturnal cough, wheeze, and get peak flow diary or spirometry.
- **Prescribing oral antihistamines for congestion** — they do not reduce nasal obstruction; use INCS.
- **Using topical decongestants beyond 5-7 days** — causes rhinitis medicamentosa (rebound swelling worse than before).
- **Diagnosing “allergic” based on peripheral eosinophilia** — eosinophils can rise in parasitic infections, drug reactions; nasal smear is more specific.
- **Not checking for aspirin sensitivity** in adults with severe rhinitis and polyps — four-phase syndrome (rhinitis, polyps, asthma, aspirin intolerance).
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call your senior (or ENT / respiratory if specialist):**

- **Acute sinusitis complicating rhinitis** — especially if worsening headache, fever >38.5°C, periorbital edema or tenderness → risk of orbital cellulitis or intracranial abscess. Urgent CT sinuses + ENT referral.
- **Asthma exacerbation triggered by allergen exposure** — if peak flow < 50% predicted, unable to complete sentences, SpO₂ < 92% on room air → start oxygen 15 L/min, salbutamol neb 5 mg q20min, call medical senior / ICU if no response after 3 nebs. Use NEWS2.
- **Anaphylaxis** — acute onset urticaria, angioedema (lips, tongue, throat), stridor, hypotension, syncope → IM adrenaline 0.5 mg (0.5 mL of 1:1000) anterolateral thigh, call crash team, escalate to ICU.
- **Sudden severe short-term (e.g., after immunotherapy injection)** — suspect systemic allergic reaction; give adrenaline, call senior.

**Specific thresholds to trigger escalation:**

| Parameter | Threshold | Action |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| NEWS2 | ≥5 | Immediate review by clinical team; consider HDU |
| SpO₂ | <92% on room air | Start oxygen, check ABG, call senior |
| Respiratory rate | >24/min | Assess for asthma/COPD exacerbation, sepsis |
| Heart rate | >110 bpm (or <50 bpm) | Check for hypovolaemia, sepsis, arrhythmia |
| Systolic BP | <90 mmHg (or drop >40 from baseline) | IV fluids, consider anaphylaxis, call ICU |
| GCS | <15 or drop by ≥2 | Hypoxia, sedation, impending respiratory failure |
| Peak flow | <50% predicted after initial bronchodilator | Admit, IV steroids, monitor in ward |
| Lactate (if sepsis suspected) | >2 mmol/L | Fluid bolus, blood cultures, escalate |
| Temperature | >39°C in patient with sinusitis | Check for orbital cellulitis, get CT sinuses |
| Mental state change / confusion | Any | Sepsis, hypoxia, meningitis; call senior |

**Final thought for the junior doctor:**  
Allergic rhinitis is a front-door diagnosis — you see it in general practice, A&E, and on the wards. Don’t dismiss it as trivial. It is a sentinel condition for atopic disease. Master the pattern recognition: itches, sneezes, runs, blocks, and always asks “where is the asthma?” Your management starts with intranasal steroids, and your escalation ends with immunotherapy. The highest-yield single action you can take today: ask every patient with rhinitis “do you also get wheezy?” — you will find undiagnosed asthma in at least one in three.

## Allergic Rhinitis
**One-liner:** IgE-mediated nasal inflammation to inhaled allergens; affects ~20% of people, linked to asthma, sinusitis, and impaired quality of life.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See
**Classic vignette:**
A 24-year-old medical student presents in late April with two weeks of sneezing, clear rhinorrhea, bilateral nasal itching, and watery eyes. Symptoms are worst in the morning and when outdoors. They have no fever, no purulent discharge, and a history of eczema as a child. On exam, you see “allergic shiners” (dark circles under the eyes) and a transverse nasal crease from rubbing their nose upward — the “allergic salute.”

**Dissecting the vignette:**

- **Seasonal timing (late April)** → tree pollen (birch, oak) trigger; points to seasonal allergic rhinitis (SAR)
- **Bilateral symptoms** → indicates systemic hypersensitivity, not local structural problem
- **Clear rhinorrhea + sneezing** → histamine-mediated hypersecretion and nerve irritation
- **Itchy eyes** → ocular allergy often co-occurs (allergic conjunctivitis)
- **Morning predominance** → overnight allergen exposure (pillow dust mites) + diurnal cortisol dip enhances inflammation
- **Eczema history** → atopic march: eczema → food allergy → asthma → allergic rhinitis
- **Allergic shiners & nasal crease** → chronic nasal congestion → venous stasis (shiners) + repeated upward rubbing (crease)

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**

1. **Isolated cough** — without sneezing or rhinorrhea; often misdiagnosed as post-nasal drip or asthma; clue: cough worse with allergen exposure, bilateral wheeze may be absent.
2. **Perennial symptoms with no seasonal pattern** — dust mite or mold allergy; patient thinks they have “chronic colds”; clue: symptoms persist year-round, worse in bedrooms, improve on holiday away from home.
3. **Nasal congestion only** — no itching, no sneezing; mimics vasomotor rhinitis or structural obstruction; clue: response to nasal steroids but not antihistamines.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)
**Plain-language definition:**
Allergic rhinitis is an inflammation of the inside of the nose triggered by an overreaction of the immune system to normally harmless particles in the air — like pollen, dust mites, or pet dander. This causes sneezing, a runny or blocked nose, and itching.

**Why it matters clinically:**

- **Frequency:** Affects up to 20-30% of adults and 40% of children in Western countries — commonest chronic disease in paediatrics.
- **Stakes:** Not “just hay fever.” It associates with asthma (two-thirds of asthmatics have rhinitis), sleep disturbance, impaired concentration, school/work absenteeism, and can worsen sinusitis, otitis media, and nasal polyps.
- **Common pitfalls:** Mistaking for recurrent viral infections leading to unnecessary antibiotics; over-prescribing oral decongestants that cause rebound congestion; failing to escalate to immunotherapy in patients with asthma or poor QoL.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings
**Story of disease development:**

1. **Sensitization phase (asymptomatic):** Inhalant allergen (e.g., birch pollen protein) is processed by dendritic cells in nasal mucosa → presented to naïve T-cells → Th2 differentiation → IL-4, IL-13 production → B-cell class-switching to IgE → IgE binds to mast cells and basophils.

2. **Early-phase response (minutes):** Re-exposure to same allergen cross-links surface IgE on mast cells → degranulation → release of histamine, tryptase, prostaglandins, leukotrienes.
   - **Mechanism → Sign:**
     - Histamine on H1 receptors in sensory nerves → *sneezing, itching*
     - Histamine on nasal glands + vasodilation → *profuse clear rhinorrhea*
     - Vasodilation + increased permeability → *congestion, edema*

3. **Late-phase response (4-6 hours):** Chemotaxis of eosinophils, neutrophils, T-cells → ongoing inflammation → epithelial damage, eosinophil activation → *persistent congestion, hyperresponsiveness* (symptoms triggered by non-allergic stimuli like cold air or perfume).

**Connecting to pattern recognition cues:**

- **Bilateral symptoms** → because allergen contacting both nasal cavities simultaneously; systemic IgE sensitization not unilateral.
- **Seasonal onset** → reflects short-lived pollen seasons; perennial allergens (dust mites) present year-round but worsen with humidity.
- **Morning worsening** → dust mites from bedding; also circadian variation in cortisol (low early morning) amplifies mast cell responsiveness.
- **Allergic shiners** → chronic venous congestion from nasal edema impairing periorbital venous drainage.
- **Nasal crease** → repeated “allergic salute” — mechanical trauma from rubbing upward.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Common Triggers | Stem Clue to Identify |
|----------|----------------|-----------------------|
| **Seasonal** | Tree pollen (spring), grass pollen (summer), weed pollen (autumn) | Symptoms appear only in specific months; travel to different region changes symptoms. |
| **Perennial** | House dust mites (Dermatophagoides pteronyssinus), moulds (Aspergillus, Alternaria), cockroach droppings, pet dander (cat, dog) | Year-round symptoms, worse in bedroom/bathroom (mould) or after cleaning; improve on holiday away from home. |
| **Occupational** | Flour (bakers), wood dust (carpenters), latex, isocyanates (painters), lab animals | Symptoms at work, resolve on weekends/holidays. |
| **Co-factor triggers** | Strong odours (perfume, cigarette smoke), cold air, exercise — these provoke symptoms in already inflamed mucosa (non-allergic hyperreactivity) | Patient says “I react to everything” — suggests late-phase inflammation is established. |

**Most common cause overall:** House dust mites (especially in humid climates). For seasonal, grass pollen is the top trigger in many regions.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means
**Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
**ARIA (Allergic Rhinitis and its Impact on Asthma) classification** — split by duration and severity:
- **Intermittent** (<4 days/week or <4 weeks) vs **Persistent** (≥4 days/week and ≥4 weeks)
- **Mild** (normal sleep, no impairment of daily activities) vs **Moderate-Severe** (abnormal sleep, impairment of work/school/leisure)

No formal scoring threshold; diagnosis is clinical + confirmatory testing.

**Key tests:**

| Test | What It Shows | Why Order It | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|--------------|----------------|
| **Skin Prick Test (SPT)** | Immediate wheal-and-flare to allergen extract applied to forearm | Confirms specific IgE sensitisation; cheap, quick, highly sensitive | Wheal ≥3 mm than negative control = positive. Must use positive (histamine) and negative (saline) controls. Interpret with clinical history — sensitisation may not equal disease. |
| **Serum specific IgE (RAST/ImmunoCAP)** | Quantity of IgE to a specific allergen | When skin testing unavailable, or if severe eczema/dermographism prevents SPT | >0.35 kU/L positive; higher levels correlate with stronger clinical reactivity but not linear. |
| **Nasal smear for eosinophils** | Eosinophils >10% of total cells on stained smear | Points to allergic inflammation rather than infective or vasomotor rhinitis | Positive strongly supports allergic rhinitis; negative does not rule out. |
| **CT sinuses** | Mucosal thickening, blockage of osteomeatal complex | Only if suspicion of chronic rhinosinusitis or complications (polyps, sinusitis) | Not first-line for uncomplicated rhinitis. |

**Pattern-recognition single finding that confirms:**
A positive SPT to a relevant allergen *combined with* symptom diary showing temporal correlation (e.g., positive grass pollen SPT + symptoms in June) is diagnostic. **Single finding** that strongly supports: nasal smear with eosinophils — but not 100% sensitive.

**What rules out:** Absence of any IgE sensitisation (negative SPT + negative specific IgE to all common allergens) makes allergic rhinitis very unlikely → consider non-allergic rhinitis, structural causes.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning
**Immediate (first hour — acute exacerbation or first presentation):**
- **Oral antihistamine:** Cetirizine 10 mg PO once daily (or loratadine 10 mg PO, less sedating). *Why:* H1-blockade rapidly reduces sneezing, itching, rhinorrhea within 1 hour. Use for 7-14 days as needed; start with second-generation to avoid drowsiness.
- **Intranasal corticosteroid (INCS):** Fluticasone furoate 55 mcg (2 sprays each nostril) once daily. *Why:* Most effective for congestion and late-phase inflammation. Takes days to work, but start immediately for EARLY control. If symptoms are mild, may start antihistamine first.

**What NOT to do in the first hour:** Do not give a topical decongestant (e.g., oxymetazoline) for >3 days — causes rebound rhinitis medicamentosa. Do not start antibiotics.

**Ward (24-72h — if hospitalized for exacerbation, e.g., with asthma flare):**
- **Intranasal corticosteroids** (continue above)
- **Saline nasal irrigation** (e.g., NeilMed sinus rinse) 2-4 times daily — mechanically clears allergens, reduces mucus viscosity, improves INCS penetration.
- **Add leukotriene receptor antagonist** (montelukast 10 mg PO nocte) if patient has concomitant asthma or inadequate symptom control after 2 weeks of INCS alone.
- **Consider oral corticosteroid** (prednisolone 30-40 mg PO daily for 5 days) only in severe cases with massive polyps or acute sinusitis. *Why:* reduces severe inflammation, but risks systemic side effects.

**Long-term (outpatient, chronic management):**
- **Stepwise escalation ladder:**  
  1. Allergen avoidance (mite-proof pillow/mattress covers, dehumidifier <50%, remove carpet, keep pets out of bedroom).  
  2. First-line prophylactic therapy: intranasal corticosteroid (fluticasone furoate 55 mcg – 2 sprays per nostril daily, or mometasone furoate 100 mcg – 2 sprays each nostril daily).  
  3. If inadequate response after 2-4 weeks: add oral antihistamine (e.g., cetirizine 10 mg daily) or intranasal antihistamine (azelastine 1 spray each nostril twice daily — works faster than INCS for itch/sneeze).  
  4. If still uncontrolled: consider immunotherapy (sublingual tablets or subcutaneous injections) for specific allergens (grass, dust mite, ragweed). *Why:* modifies immune response, induces tolerance, can cure disease. Duration 3-5 years.  
- **Ophthalmic allergy:** Olopatadine 0.1% eye drops (1 drop in each eye BD) for itch, redness.

**Common pitfalls to avoid:**
- Using oral antihistamines alone for congestion — they do little for obstruction. INCS is best.
- Prescribing decongestant sprays for >7 days | leads to drug-induced rhinitis.
- Forgetting to assess for asthma in every patient with allergic rhinitis — recommend peak flow diary and spirometry.
- Not escalating to immunotherapy in patients with poor QoL or new-onset asthma despite pharmacotherapy.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Allergic Rhinitis | Non-Allergic Rhinitis (Vasomotor) | Chronic Rhinosinusitis (CRS) | Nasal Polyps |
|---------|-------------------|-----------------------------------|------------------------------|--------------|
| **Itching** | Prominent (nose, eyes, palate) | Minimal or absent | Usually absent | Absent |
| **Sneezing** | Frequent, paroxysmal | Occasional | Uncommon | Rare |
| **Rhinorrhea** | Clear, watery | Clear or serous | Thick, purulent possible | Watery or thick |
| **Nasal obstruction** | Mild-moderate, fluctuates | Often prominent (trigger-dependent) | Moderate-severe, fixed | Severe, bilateral, progressive |
| **Seasonal pattern** | Yes (allergen-specific) | No (triggered by irritants: smoke, cold air, heat) | No | No |
| **Facial pain/pressure** | Rare | Rare | Common (over maxillary sinuses) | May occur with sinusitis |
| **Olfactory loss** | Mild, reversible | Mild | Moderate | Prominent, often early |
| **Response to antihistamines** | Good (early-phase sx) | Poor | Poor | Poor |
| **Response to nasal steroids** | Good | Variable | Good (as adjunct) | Good (reduces size) |
| **Key diagnostic test** | Skin prick test + specific IgE positive | Negative allergy tests; challenge with external triggers | CT sinuses (mucosal thickening, air-fluid levels) | CT sinuses (soft-tissue density filling nasal cavity); endoscopy shows grape-like masses |
| **Key differentiator** | Itching + sneezing + allergen-specific IgE | Absence of itching, no eosinophilia on smear | Purulent drainage + facial pain + CT findings | Anosmia + visible polyps on exam |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?
**1. Acute Sinusitis**
- **Presentation:** Persistent purulent nasal discharge, unilateral facial pain over maxillary/ frontal sinuses, fever, headache worse on bending.
- **Key signs:** Tenderness on percussion over sinuses, nasal endoscopy showing pus from middle meatus.
- **What to do:** Treat with amoxicillin 500 mg TDS PO for 7-10 days (or co-amoxiclav if previous antibiotics). Add saline irrigation, mucolytics. If recurrent, refer ENT.

**2. Asthma Exacerbation**
- **Presentation:** Deteriorating peak flow, nocturnal cough, wheeze, chest tightness during pollen season.
- **Key signs:** Bilateral expiratory wheeze, reduced FEV1/FVC ratio <0.7.
- **What to do:** Optimize rhinitis treatment (INCS + antihistamine) → the nose controls the lower airway. In acute: β2-agonist (salbutamol 2 puffs PRN every 20 minutes for 2 hours, then as needed) + oral prednisolone 40 mg daily for 5 days. If severe, escalate to senior.

**3. Otitis Media with Effusion (Glue Ear)**
- **Presentation:** Hearing loss, ear fullness, delayed speech in children; not painful.
- **Key signs:** Dull tympanic membrane, reduced mobility on pneumatic otoscopy, B-type tympanogram.
- **What to do:** Treat rhinitis aggressively (INCS, antihistamine). If persists >3 months, refer ENT for grommets.

**4. Nasal Polyps (late-presentation pattern)**
- **Pattern:** Patient with long-standing untreated allergic rhinitis (or non-allergic eosinophilic disease) develops progressive anosmia, constant nasal obstruction not responding to decongestants; exam shows pale greyish, grape-like masses.
- **What to do:** Start INCS high dose (e.g., betamethasone nose drops or fluticasone PPP). If no improvement, refer ENT for polypectomy and consider aspirin desensitization if associated with aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease.

**5. Sleep Apnoea / Impaired QoL**
- Chronic congestion → mouth breathing → obstructive sleep apnoea (snoring, daytime somnolence). Treat rhinitis → improve CPAP compliance.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls
**Mnemonics:**

1. **"ARIA" classification** — *A*llergic *R*hinitis *I*mpact on *A*sthma → severity: Mild, Moderate-Severe; duration: Intermittent, Persistent.
2. **"SPIKES"** for symptoms: *S*neezing, *P*runny nose (rhinorrhea), *I*tching (nose, eyes, palate), *K*ongestion (nasal obstruction), *E*ye watering, *S*mell loss (partial).
3. **Common seasonal allergens by month** (Northern temperate): "Tree in Spring, Grass in Summer, Weed in Fall" — mnemonic: **T**ree, **G**rass, **W**eed → **T**he **G**arden **W**ilts.

**High-yield clinical pearls:**

- **The “allergic salute”** — upward rubbing of the nose with palm, causing a transverse nasal crease (Dennie-Morgan line variant). Seen in children and adults with chronic rhinitis.
- **Allergic shiners** — dark periorbital circles due to venous stasis from nasal congestion; not pathognomonic but strongly supportive of allergic rhinitis.
- **The “hourglass” pattern of congestion** — alternating nasal obstruction between nostrils (nasal cycle) is exaggerated in allergic rhinitis; if obstruction is fixed, suspect structural cause (deviated septum, polyps).
- **Ocular involvement** — if a patient denies eye symptoms but sneezes profusely, ask “do your eyes water or itch?” often they didn't think to mention it because it’s mild.
- **“Allergy skin test” is diagnostic, but interpret cautiously** — + test + history = disease; + test without symptoms = sensitization without clinical allergy (common in atopic individuals).
- **The key message for patients:** “Treat the nose early, and you can improve your asthma and prevent sinus infections.”
- **When to suspect non-allergic rhinitis:** no itching, no sneezing, no eye symptoms, negative allergy tests, trigger is irritant (e.g., perfume, cold air, spicy food). Treatment: intranasal ipratropium for rhinorrhea or intranasal azelastine/fluticasone combination.

**Common exam / ward mistakes to avoid:**

- **Missing asthma** in a rhinitis patient — do not assume “just hay fever”; ask about nocturnal cough, wheeze, and get peak flow diary or spirometry.
- **Prescribing oral antihistamines for congestion** — they do not reduce nasal obstruction; use INCS.
- **Using topical decongestants beyond 5-7 days** — causes rhinitis medicamentosa (rebound swelling worse than before).
- **Diagnosing “allergic” based on peripheral eosinophilia** — eosinophils can rise in parasitic infections, drug reactions; nasal smear is more specific.
- **Not checking for aspirin sensitivity** in adults with severe rhinitis and polyps — four-phase syndrome (rhinitis, polyps, asthma, aspirin intolerance).

### 10. Escalation Criteria
**When to call your senior (or ENT / respiratory if specialist):**

- **Acute sinusitis complicating rhinitis** — especially if worsening headache, fever >38.5°C, periorbital edema or tenderness → risk of orbital cellulitis or intracranial abscess. Urgent CT sinuses + ENT referral.
- **Asthma exacerbation triggered by allergen exposure** — if peak flow < 50% predicted, unable to complete sentences, SpO₂ < 92% on room air → start oxygen 15 L/min, salbutamol neb 5 mg q20min, call medical senior / ICU if no response after 3 nebs. Use NEWS2.
- **Anaphylaxis** — acute onset urticaria, angioedema (lips, tongue, throat), stridor, hypotension, syncope → IM adrenaline 0.5 mg (0.5 mL of 1:1000) anterolateral thigh, call crash team, escalate to ICU.
- **Sudden severe short-term (e.g., after immunotherapy injection)** — suspect systemic allergic reaction; give adrenaline, call senior.

**Specific thresholds to trigger escalation:**

| Parameter | Threshold | Action |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| NEWS2 | ≥5 | Immediate review by clinical team; consider HDU |
| SpO₂ | <92% on room air | Start oxygen, check ABG, call senior |
| Respiratory rate | >24/min | Assess for asthma/COPD exacerbation, sepsis |
| Heart rate | >110 bpm (or <50 bpm) | Check for hypovolaemia, sepsis, arrhythmia |
| Systolic BP | <90 mmHg (or drop >40 from baseline) | IV fluids, consider anaphylaxis, call ICU |
| GCS | <15 or drop by ≥2 | Hypoxia, sedation, impending respiratory failure |
| Peak flow | <50% predicted after initial bronchodilator | Admit, IV steroids, monitor in ward |
| Lactate (if sepsis suspected) | >2 mmol/L | Fluid bolus, blood cultures, escalate |
| Temperature | >39°C in patient with sinusitis | Check for orbital cellulitis, get CT sinuses |
| Mental state change / confusion | Any | Sepsis, hypoxia, meningitis; call senior |

**Final thought for the junior doctor:**  
Allergic rhinitis is a front-door diagnosis — you see it in general practice, A&E, and on the wards. Don’t dismiss it as trivial. It is a sentinel condition for atopic disease. Master the pattern recognition: itches, sneezes, runs, blocks, and always asks “where is the asthma?” Your management starts with intranasal steroids, and your escalation ends with immunotherapy. The highest-yield single action you can take today: ask every patient with rhinitis “do you also get wheezy?” — you will find undiagnosed asthma in at least one in three.
